critique.summary: The article criticizes a PC Gamer webpage for its excessive advertising and large data usage, highlighting that the page initially loads 37MB and continues to download more ads, accumulating nearly half a gigabyte in a few minutes. It also mentions the usefulness of RSS readers in avoiding such intrusive content.
mainPoints: The PC Gamer article page displays multiple popups and at least five visible ads upon loading.
The initial page load size is approximately 37MB, which is unusually large for a web article.
The website continues to download additional ads over time, reaching nearly half a gigabyte within five minutes.
RSS readers like NetNewsWire, Unread, Current, and Reeder can help users bypass such intrusive advertising and large data downloads.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the page loading multiple ads and popups are plausible and consistent with common web practices on ad-supported sites. The reported 37MB initial load size and continued downloading of ads totaling nearly half a gigabyte are unusually high but technically possible, especially if the site loads many high-resolution images, videos, or scripts. However, without direct measurement or corroboration from independent sources, these specific data size claims cannot be fully verified. The recommendation of RSS readers as a way to avoid such issues is accurate and widely recognized.
considerations: The article does not provide technical details or tools used to measure the data usage, which would strengthen the claims.
It does not discuss the potential reasons for such high data usage, such as video ads or auto-playing media.
The article does not consider user settings or ad blockers that might mitigate the problem.
No perspectives from PC Gamer or the website operators are included to explain or respond to these issues.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the excessive data usage and ad bloat of a PC Gamer article recommending RSS readers, with commenters highlighting the large download sizes, the impact of autoplaying ads, and the general poor user experience on ad-heavy websites. Some suggest technical solutions like ad blockers and script control, while others discuss the broader implications for web usability and privacy.

comments.author: MBCook
comments.text:
The title buried the lede.

> In the five minutes since I started writing this post the website has downloaded almost half a gigabyte of new ads.

I’m guessing this is due to autoplaying videos. *500 MB* in 5 minutes.

37 MB is petite compared to that.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a concrete observation about the scale of data usage, attributing it plausibly to autoplaying videos, which aligns with common causes of heavy ad data consumption.

comments.author: userbinator
comments.text: To use a good point of reference that I've seen others also start using lately, an installation of Windows 95 is roughly 40MB, so in loading that page you've downloaded approximately one Windows 95 installation. Then another 10+ times with the 500MB more that came after.
comments.analysis: This comment uses a relatable analogy to effectively illustrate the excessive data usage, which is consistent with the reported download sizes.

comments.author: WarOnPrivacy
comments.text:
In Firefox + Unlock Origin: Downloads 5.6MB and then stops loading.

Scrolling to the bottom of the page added 3MB of images and then stopped loading.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a practical test result using privacy tools, showing significantly reduced data usage, supporting the effectiveness of ad blockers.

comments.author: djoldman
comments.text:
I can't recommend enough limiting JS to an allowlist.

By default, I browse without JS. If I get to a website that I want to explore that requires JS, I turn it on with one click:

https://github.com/maximelebreton/quick-javascript-switcher
comments.analysis: Offers a practical and effective solution to reduce data bloat and improve browsing experience by controlling JavaScript execution.

comments.author: elorant
comments.text: Thank God for uMatrix. Seriously, I don't know how I lived without that thing. Load times on everything are at least 30% faster.
comments.analysis: Recommends a specific tool known for blocking unwanted content, which is a valid approach to mitigating excessive data use.

comments.author: goldenarm
comments.text:
I'm trying to migrate to 100% RSS right now, to avoid the hateful algorithmic editorialization of modern social media.

And I'm shocked that almost no paid media provides full articles in RSS anymore, and force me to navigate their 37MB pages with popups all over the place. Has anyone found a solution against that ?

Edit : Sorry I'm asking specifically about paywalled stuff
comments.analysis: Highlights a relevant user experience issue with paywalled content and RSS feeds, reflecting a common frustration among users.

comments.author: notepad0x90
comments.text:
we need some sort of a universal crowd-sourced site rating system. Things like user experience, scamminess, user-hostility, site ownership-affiliations,etc.. all opt-in by users of course, you setup the criteria that is important to you and the browser displays different ratings or blocks certain sites (like scammy/fraudulent ones) out right. The reputation providers would also be selectable like search engines. I'd imagine there would be crowdsourced lists of all sorts.

If you have older pepople struggling with cognition for example, this would be a good way to limit their exposure to scams.

But commercial sites like this could also be rated as a privacy risk for the intense ad capitalism, or a 'bloat' to tell users it will slow down their computer by visiting the site. You could set it up so that when certain categories and ratings are met, the browser warns you before you could navigate to it.

Another idea is to have this same system include alternative suggestions. For example, if a site has age verification, you would be able to setup your browser so that it warns you when you visit sites of that nature, listing alternatives recommended by the list maintainer, for whatever that site provides.
comments.analysis: Proposes an innovative but complex solution to improve web browsing safety and quality, showing forward-thinking but requiring significant implementation.

comments.author: __natty__
comments.text:
It's not just "PC Gamer" but people making decisions behind as always. Three first people from their "Meet the Team" page [0]: Tim Clark — Brand Director (@timothydclark), Evan Lahti - Strategic Director (@elahti), Phil Savage — Global Editor-in-Chief (@Octaeder). Hopefully they can see this HN thread and people complains and do "something" about that.

[0] https://www.pcgamer.com/meet-the-team/
comments.analysis: Points out accountability by naming responsible individuals, which is relevant but does not address technical issues directly.

comments.author: touwer
comments.text: Even more embarassing is that the article adds really nothing to whatever was written before about rss. Probably gobbled up by AI
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration but offers an unsubstantiated claim about AI involvement, reducing credibility.

comments.author: mnkyprskbd
comments.text: At this point, if you browse the internet without an adblock; it is on YOU.
comments.analysis: Blames users for lack of ad blocking, which is dismissive and overlooks broader systemic issues.
